FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, release 5.1, population-based longitudinal cohort, 21 U.S. sites, baseline ages 9-10 years (2016-2022).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cohort study; latent class linear mixed models on ABCD data; prospective associations of addictive use trajectories with suicidal behaviors, suicidal ideation, and CBCL outcomes at year 4.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed original investigation in JAMA (2025); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) and MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire) are qualifying phone/SM variables used as IVs predicting individual-trait-level outcomes. Adjusted Poisson RRs reported: increasing SMAQ trajectory -> suicidal behaviors RR = 2.14 (95% CI, 1.61-2.85); high MPIQ trajectory -> suicidal behaviors RR = 2.17 (95% CI, 1.48-3.19); both also predict suicidal ideation and CBCL internalizing/externalizing T scores (mean differences reported in Figure 3). Outcomes are individual-trait-level (suicidal behaviors, suicidal ideation, internalizing/externalizing symptoms).

FT_IC3.evidence: Abundant quantitative results: risk ratios with 95% CIs for suicidal behaviors and suicidal ideation, and mean differences for CBCL T scores, all reported in Figure 3 and text for each trajectory type.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses performed: Poisson regression for categorical outcomes, generalized linear models for continuous outcomes; all results quantified with effect estimates and 95% CIs.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone (SMAQ, MPIQ) is the IV predicting mental health outcomes (suicidal behaviors, ideation, CBCL symptoms); EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis, which is not the case here.
notes: VGAQ (video game addictive use) also analyzed and reported separately; SMAQ and MPIQ alone are sufficient for IC2. Total screen time at baseline analyzed as a sensitivity comparison but found non-significant; primary qualifying exposure is platform-specific addictive use trajectories.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 21 sites across the United States, baseline ages 9-10 years; data from year 2 (ages 11-13) and year 4 (ages 13-15) follow-ups; final sample n=543 from original n=10,964.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using linear mixed models on ABCD data; examines VGA and SMA associations with fMRI-measured neural reward/loss sensitivity (MID task) over two time points.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Computers in Human Behavior (2025); no indication of duplicate or preprint of already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) is a qualifying SM variable. Model 1 (Table 3) reports numeric estimates (b coefficients, 95% CIs, p values) for SMA x Time interactions on neural beta weights (fMRI MID task responses in nucleus accumbens, amygdala, insula, ACC, OFC, vmPFC) — individual-trait-level neural outcomes. For example, SMA x Time interaction on right insula loss contrast: b = -0.081, 95% CI [-0.15, -0.02], p = .017 (uncorrected; pFDR = 0.089). These are numeric estimates pairing SMAQ with neural individual-trait variables. Significance is not required for IC2 — the estimates exist and are reported. Model 2 (Table 4) also tests whether neural loss/reward sensitivity predicts VGA (a qualifying variable, though gaming; but SMA also included as DV for completeness). The qualifying pairing is SMAQ as IV -> neural fMRI outcomes as individual-trait DV throughout all main LMMs.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric estimates (standardized beta coefficients, 95% CIs, uncorrected and FDR-corrected p values) reported throughout Tables 2, 3, and 4 for both VGA and SMA associations with neural outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential LMMs with reported beta coefficients, 95% CIs; Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction applied; paired-samples t-tests and simple slope analyses also reported.

FT_EC7_reason: SMAQ (SM variable) appears as IV in Model 1 predicting neural outcomes; EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis, which is not the case. SM is IV in the primary longitudinal models.
notes: VGAQ is the primary finding (significant bidirectional relationship with neural loss sensitivity); SMA showed no significant longitudinal effects after FDR correction. However, SMAQ is explicitly included as an IV in all LMMs with reported numeric estimates against neural individual-trait outcomes, satisfying IC2 Step 1. The qualifying SM variable is SMAQ; VGA findings are incidental to eligibility.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cohort data from ABCD Study Release 5.1; participants at 2-year follow-up (T2, ages 11-12, n=9,054) and 4-year follow-up (T4, ages 13-14, n=4,007); 21 US sites; coordinating center at University of California.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional network analysis (Gaussian graphical models) and cross-lagged panel-network (CLPN) models using ABCD data to compare centrality of problematic screen use vs. screen time in developmental networks.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed full-length report in Journal of Behavioral Addictions (2025); no indication of duplicate or preprint of already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: SMAQ (Problematic Social Media, node A2) and MPIQ (Problematic Mobile Phone, node A3) are qualifying SM/phone variables with reported network edge weights (partial correlations) paired with individual-trait-level nodes including psychopathological symptoms (anxious/depressed, withdrawn/depressed, somatic complaints, social problems, thought problems, attention problems, rule-breaking, aggressive behavior) and behavioral tendencies (UPPS impulsiveness, BIS/BAS). Specific numeric estimates in Figure 3 and text: female network, problematic social media (A2) -> behavioral activation (BAS), B = 0.179; A2 -> delinquent peer involvement (A2->E3), B = 0.108; MPIQ (A3) -> impulsiveness (UPPS), B = 0.219; A3 -> behavioral activation (BAS), B = 0.194; A3 -> behavioral inhibition (BIS), B = 0.108. Cross-lagged effects also reported. Individual-trait outcomes (psychopathology, impulsiveness, behavioral inhibition/activation) satisfy the other-side requirement.

FT_IC3.evidence: Network edge weights (partial correlation coefficients, B values with 95% CIs), bootstrapped difference tests, and CLPN cross-lagged coefficients (B with 95% CIs) reported throughout Figures 2-4 and text.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential network analysis (GGM with gLASSO, bootstrapped centrality differences) and CLPN analyses; all quantitative results reported with 95% CI values.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional carve-out applies: the paper contains cross-sectional network analyses where SM/phone variables (SMAQ, MPIQ) appear as nodes with edges to other variables (functioning as both IV and DV in the undirected network). Additionally, in CLPN analyses, problematic SM/phone use is both a predictor of future outcomes (IV role) and predicted by prior factors (DV role), so SM is not DV-only.
notes: Screen time variables (B1 video game time, B2 social media time, B3 phone checking) are also included as nodes. TV time is included as a screen use node in the network but SMAQ and MPIQ are separately measured and provide the qualifying IC2 estimates. The composite screen time measure does not need to satisfy IC2 because SMAQ/MPIQ individually qualify under Step 1.
